sections.title: 1. Introduction: Multi-Agent Systems and Game Theory

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Algorithmic Game Theory (AGT)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: AGT studies algorithms in strategic environments where inputs come from self-interested agents
AGT focuses exclusively on mechanism design, ignoring equilibrium computation and MARL
AGT combines tools from theoretical computer science and economics
AGT application areas include resource allocation, mechanism design, equilibrium computation, and multiagent RL
AGT was developed to eliminate strategic behaviour from multi-agent systems

explanations.text: This is the core definition of AGT given in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: the study of algorithms in strategic environments where the inputs come from self-interested agents

explanations.text: AGT covers four areas: resource allocation, mechanism design, equilibrium computation, and MARL. It is not limited to mechanism design alone.
AGT explicitly integrates tools from both fields to analyse equilibria and design mechanisms.
explanations.notes_quote: It combines tools from theoretical computer science and economics

explanations.text: All four areas are listed as key AGT application areas in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Resource Allocation and Scheduling... Mechanism Design for Incentives... Equilibrium Computation in Networked Agents... Multiagent Reinforcement Learning

explanations.text: AGT analyses and designs algorithms in the PRESENCE of strategic behaviour — it studies it, not eliminates it.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Computational Social Choice (COMSOC)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: COMSOC studies collective decision-making and preference aggregation using tools from CS, economics, and social choice theory
COMSOC is a subfield of AGT concerned exclusively with two-player auction design
COMSOC application areas include fair division, ranking and recommendation, and participatory budgeting
COMSOC asks how individual preferences can be combined into a collective decision while accounting for computational issues
COMSOC is concerned only with competitive, self-interested agents and ignores collective welfare

explanations.text: This matches the definition given in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: an interdisciplinary field that studies collective decision-making and preference aggregation using tools from computer science, economics, and social choice theory

explanations.text: COMSOC is a separate interdisciplinary field, not a subfield of AGT, and covers far more than auction design: fair division, ranking, participatory budgeting, and collective decisions.
These three are among the four key COMSOC application areas listed in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Fair Division... Ranking and Recommendation... Participatory Budgeting and Resource Assignment

explanations.text: This is the central question of COMSOC, explicitly stated in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: how a group's individual preferences over alternatives can be combined into a collective decision, while explicitly accounting for computational issues

explanations.text: COMSOC is focused on collective welfare and group decision-making — it is not limited to competitive settings.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) and non-cooperative games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A MAS is composed of multiple interacting software components (agents) that may be cooperative or competitive
The competitive case in MAS is modelled as a cooperative game with binding contracts among players
In a non-cooperative game, players strategically optimise their own utility functions
MAS agents can only model artificial software — not humans, companies, or nations
Non-cooperative games assume no binding contracts among players

explanations.text: The notes define MAS as a system of multiple interacting agents which may be cooperative or competitive.
explanations.notes_quote: a system composed of multiple interacting software components (agents), which may be: Cooperative... Competitive (self-interested)

explanations.text: The competitive case is modelled as a NON-cooperative game — there are no binding contracts among players.
In the competitive MAS case, each agent optimises its own utility function.
explanations.notes_quote: agents strategically optimise their own utility functions

explanations.text: The notes state that a player is a general entity: individual, company, nation, protocol, animal, or artificial agent.
This is a defining property of non-cooperative games.
explanations.notes_quote: there are no binding contracts among the players

questions.text: Which of the following are correct properties of a game in normal (strategic) form? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A normal form game requires at least 2 players; a player is a general entity such as an individual, company, nation, or AI agent
Each player has a preference relation modelled as a utility (payoff) function over possible outcomes
An outcome is determined solely by the single player who moves first
Strategies are actions a player chooses to follow; an outcome is determined by the mutual choice of strategies
A normal form game requires exactly 2 players

explanations.text: The notes say 'at least 2 players' and list individual, company, nation, protocol, animal, artificial agent as examples.
explanations.notes_quote: At least 2 PLAYERS — a player is a general entity: individual, company, nation, protocol, animal, artificial agent

explanations.text: Each player's preferences are modelled via a utility/payoff function.
explanations.notes_quote: A PREFERENCE RELATION for each player over possible outcomes — modeled as a utility (or payoff) function

explanations.text: An outcome is determined by the MUTUAL choice of strategies — all players contribute to the outcome.
Strategies are actions players choose, and outcomes depend on the combined strategy profile.
explanations.notes_quote: strategies are actions which a player chooses to follow, and an outcome is determined by mutual choice of strategies

explanations.text: The definition requires AT LEAST 2 players, not exactly 2. Games can have any finite number $n \geq 2$.

questions.text: Given the formal game tuple $\Gamma = (\mathbf{N}, (S_i)_{i \in \mathbf{N}}, (U_i)_{i \in \mathbf{N}})$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\mathbf{N}$ is a finite set of players
$S_i$ is the set of strategies available to player $i \in \mathbf{N}$
$U_i : S \to \mathbb{R}$ maps strategy profiles to real-valued payoffs, where $S = \times_{i \in \mathbf{N}} S_i$ is the Cartesian product of all strategy sets
The set of strategy profiles is $S = \sum_{i \in \mathbf{N}} S_i$, the sum of all individual strategy sets
$S = \times_{i \in \mathbf{N}} S_i$ represents all possible strategy combinations (profiles), one strategy per player

explanations.text: $\mathbf{N}$ is explicitly defined as a finite set of players.
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathbf{N}$ is a (finite) set of players

explanations.text: Each player $i$ has their own strategy set $S_i$.
explanations.notes_quote: $S_i$ is a set of strategies for each player $i \in \mathbf{N}$

explanations.text: $U_i$ maps from the strategy profile space $S$ (the Cartesian product) to the reals.
explanations.notes_quote: $U_i : S \to \mathbb{R}$ is a utility function of $i \in \mathbf{N}$, where $S = \times_{i \in \mathbf{N}} S_i$ is the set of strategy combinations

explanations.text: $S$ is the CARTESIAN PRODUCT $\times_{i \in \mathbf{N}} S_i$, not the arithmetic sum $\sum$. The Cartesian product gives all combinations of strategies.
The Cartesian product captures all possible joint strategy choices — one strategy per player per profile.
explanations.notes_quote: $S = \times_{i \in \mathbf{N}} S_i$ is the set of strategy combinations (or profiles)

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the notation used for strategy profiles and coalitions? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A strategy profile $s = (s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n)$ is a vector of strategies, one for each player
$s_{-i}$ denotes the vector of strategies of all players except player $i$
For a two-player game, $s_{-1}$ refers to Player 1's own strategy (their self-strategy)
$s_{-C}$ denotes the strategies of players IN coalition $C$
A full strategy profile can be written as $(s_C, s_{-C})$ for any coalition $C \subseteq N$

explanations.text: A strategy profile specifies one strategy per player.
explanations.notes_quote: A strategy profile $s = (s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n)$ is a vector of strategies for all players

explanations.text: The $-i$ subscript means 'all players except $i$'.
explanations.notes_quote: $s_{-i}$ denotes the vector of all players' strategies except player $i$

explanations.text: $s_{-1}$ denotes strategies of ALL players EXCEPT Player 1 — i.e., Player 2's strategy in a two-player game.
$s_{-C}$ denotes strategies of players NOT in coalition $C$ (the complement). $s_C$ denotes strategies of players IN $C$.
The profile is split into coalition $C$'s strategies and its complement's strategies.
explanations.notes_quote: We write a profile as $(s_C, s_{-C})$

questions.text:
Given the Prisoner's Dilemma payoff matrix:

|  | C | D |
|---|---|---|
| **C** | $-1, -1$ | $-10, 0$ |
| **D** | $0, -10$ | $-5, -5$ |

Which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Both players have a dominant strategy to Defect
The unique pure strategy Nash equilibrium is (C, C)
The equilibrium (D, D) with payoffs $(-5, -5)$ is Pareto-dominated by (C, C) with payoffs $(-1, -1)$
Individual rationality leads to a collectively suboptimal outcome
The Prisoner's Dilemma has two pure strategy Nash equilibria

explanations.text: D is strictly dominant: $0 > -1$ when opponent plays C, and $-5 > -10$ when opponent plays D.
explanations.notes_quote: The only stable outcome is (D, D) — each player is better off defecting no matter what the other does

explanations.text: The unique NE is (D, D), not (C, C). (C, C) is Pareto-superior but not an equilibrium — each player wants to deviate to D.
(C, C) gives $(-1,-1)$ while (D, D) gives $(-5,-5)$. Both players are better off at (C, C), so (D, D) is Pareto-dominated.
explanations.notes_quote: The equilibrium (D,D) with payoffs $(P,P)$ is Pareto-dominated by (C,C) with payoffs $(R,R)$

explanations.text: This is the central insight of the Prisoner's Dilemma.
explanations.notes_quote: Individual rationality leads to a collectively suboptimal outcome

explanations.text: The PD has exactly ONE pure strategy NE: (D, D). Both players strictly prefer D regardless of the opponent.

questions.text: In the general form of the Prisoner's Dilemma with payoff ordering $T > R > P > S$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $T$ is the Temptation payoff: what you earn by defecting when your opponent cooperates
$R$ is the Reward for mutual cooperation
The condition $P > S$ means mutual cooperation gives a higher payoff than being the cooperating sucker
The equilibrium payoff pair $(P, P)$ is Pareto-dominated by the cooperative outcome $(R, R)$
The ordering $T > R > P > S$ is necessary to create the Prisoner's Dilemma structure

explanations.text: $T$ stands for Temptation — the payoff from defecting against a cooperating opponent.
explanations.notes_quote: T > R > P > S (Temptation > Reward > Punishment > Sucker's payoff)

explanations.text: $R$ is the Reward for mutual cooperation.
explanations.notes_quote: T > R > P > S (Temptation > Reward > Punishment > Sucker's payoff)

explanations.text: $P > S$ means mutual DEFECTION ($P$) gives more than being a sucker ($S$). The condition $R > P$ means mutual cooperation beats mutual defection.
Since $R > P$, both players prefer (C,C) with payoffs $(R,R)$ over the NE (D,D) with payoffs $(P,P)$.
explanations.notes_quote: The equilibrium (D,D) with payoffs $(P,P)$ is Pareto-dominated by (C,C) with payoffs $(R,R)$

explanations.text: This ordering is the defining condition that creates the PD structure — temptation to defect combined with punishment for mutual defection.
explanations.notes_quote: T > R > P > S (Temptation > Reward > Punishment > Sucker's payoff)

questions.source_ref: Section 3.2: The Ice Cream Wars (Hotelling's Model)
questions.text: In Hotelling's Ice Cream Wars model, two vendors choose positions on $[0, 1]$ and customers go to the nearest vendor. Which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The unique Nash equilibrium has both vendors at the centre: $s_1 = s_2 = \frac{1}{2}$
When Ed is at $\frac{1}{4}$ and Ted deviates to $\frac{1}{2}$, Ted captures $\frac{5}{8}$ and Ed retains $\frac{3}{8}$ of the market
In this model, customers travel to the farthest vendor
The strategy set for each vendor is $S_i = [0, 1]$
At the Nash equilibrium, each vendor captures $\frac{1}{4}$ of the market

explanations.text: Both vendors moving to the centre is the unique NE — neither can gain by moving away.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: The Ice Cream Wars (Hotelling's Model)
explanations.notes_quote: The unique Nash equilibrium is $s_1 = s_2 = 1/2$ (both vendors at the centre)

explanations.text: Ted's deviation from $3/4$ to $1/2$ is profitable: he gains market share from $1/2$ to $5/8$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: The Ice Cream Wars (Hotelling's Model)
explanations.notes_quote: Ted deviates to $1/2$ → Ted gets $5/8$, Ed gets $3/8$

explanations.text: Customers go to the NEAREST vendor, not the farthest.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: The Ice Cream Wars (Hotelling's Model)

explanations.text: Each player can position anywhere on the unit interval $[0,1]$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: The Ice Cream Wars (Hotelling's Model)
explanations.notes_quote: $N = \{1,2\}$ and $S_1 = S_2 = [0,1]$, where strategy $s_i \in S_i$ represents setting up a cart at position $s_i$

explanations.text: At the NE ($s_1 = s_2 = 1/2$), each vendor captures $\frac{1}{2}$ of the market (they split it equally).
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: The Ice Cream Wars (Hotelling's Model)

questions.source_ref: Section 3.3: Working on a Joint Project
questions.text:
In the joint project game:

|  | Work hard | Goof off |
|---|---|---|
| **Work hard** | $2, 2$ | $0, 3$ |
| **Goof off** | $3, 0$ | $1, 1$ |

Which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: This game has the same structure as the Prisoner's Dilemma
The dominant strategy for each player is to Goof off
(Work hard, Work hard) is the Nash equilibrium of this game
(Goof off, Goof off) is the Nash equilibrium with payoffs $(1, 1)$
The (Goof off, Goof off) equilibrium is Pareto-dominated by (Work hard, Work hard)

explanations.text: The notes explicitly state this is another instance of the Prisoner's Dilemma structure.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.3: Working on a Joint Project
explanations.notes_quote: This is another instance of the Prisoner's Dilemma structure

explanations.text: Goof off gives 3 vs 2 (opponent works) and 1 vs 0 (opponent goofs) — strictly dominant.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.3: Working on a Joint Project

explanations.text: (Work hard, Work hard) is NOT a NE — each player would deviate to Goof off (earning 3 instead of 2).
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.3: Working on a Joint Project

explanations.text: (Goof off, Goof off) is the NE since Goof off is the dominant strategy.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.3: Working on a Joint Project

explanations.text: Both players prefer $(2,2)$ over $(1,1)$, so (Work hard, Work hard) Pareto-dominates the equilibrium.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.3: Working on a Joint Project

questions.text:
In the duopoly game where two firms choose High or Low pricing:

|  | High | Low |
|---|---|---|
| **High** | $1000, 1000$ | $-200, 1200$ |
| **Low** | $1200, -200$ | $600, 600$ |

Which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: (Low, Low) is the Nash equilibrium with payoffs $(600, 600)$
(High, High) with payoffs $(1000, 1000)$ Pareto-dominates the equilibrium
This game has a different structure from the Prisoner's Dilemma
A firm choosing Low when the other chooses High earns $1200$
The equilibrium (Low, Low) maximises joint payoffs

explanations.text: Low is a dominant strategy for each firm, leading to the (Low, Low) equilibrium.
explanations.notes_quote: Again, Prisoner's Dilemma structure: (Low, Low) is the equilibrium despite (High, High) being better for both

explanations.text: Both firms earn 1000 at (High,High) vs 600 at (Low,Low) — (High,High) is Pareto-superior.
The notes explicitly say this is ANOTHER Prisoner's Dilemma: dominant strategy leads to Pareto-inferior outcome.
explanations.notes_quote: Again, Prisoner's Dilemma structure

explanations.text: From the payoff matrix, (Low, High) gives the Low firm $1200$.
(High, High) maximises joint payoffs at $2000$, compared to $1200$ at (Low, Low). The equilibrium is the LOWEST joint payoff outcome.

questions.text:
In the Professor's Dilemma:

|  | Listen | Sleep |
|---|---|---|
| **Make effort** | $(10^6, 10^6)$ | $(-10, 0)$ |
| **Slack off** | $(0, -10)$ | $(0, 0)$ |

Which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: This game has two pure strategy Nash equilibria: (Make effort, Listen) and (Slack off, Sleep)
Neither player has a dominant or dominated strategy
(Slack off, Sleep) is the unique Nash equilibrium in this game
(Make effort, Listen) gives payoffs $(10^6, 10^6)$
Each player's best strategy depends entirely on what the other player does

explanations.text: Both (Make effort, Listen) and (Slack off, Sleep) are NE — no player wants to deviate at either profile.
explanations.notes_quote: Two pure strategy Nash equilibria: (Make effort, Listen) and (Slack off, Sleep)

explanations.text: Unlike the PD, neither player has a strategy that is best regardless of the opponent.
explanations.notes_quote: No player has a dominant or dominated strategy

explanations.text: There are TWO Nash equilibria in this game, not one. (Slack off, Sleep) is a NE, but so is (Make effort, Listen).
From the payoff matrix, (Make effort, Listen) gives payoffs $(10^6, 10^6)$.
The best strategy depends on what the other player does — there is no dominant strategy.
explanations.notes_quote: The best strategy depends on what the other player is doing

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the Tragedy of the Commons? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It describes situations where individual incentives to overconsume a shared resource lead to collective loss
The phrase originates from Garrett Hardin's 1968 essay in Science, drawing on Lloyd's description of overgrazing
In MAS and AI, it appears when many agents share a limited resource and optimise only their own reward
The Tragedy of the Commons has no relevance to artificial intelligence or multi-agent systems
Overfishing, air pollution, and overgrazing on shared pastureland are examples

explanations.text: This is the definition of the social dilemma known as the Tragedy of the Commons.
explanations.notes_quote: individuals have an incentive to overconsume a common resource and act in their own self-interest at the expense of society

explanations.text: The historical origin is cited in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: comes from Garrett Hardin's 1968 essay in Science, drawing on 19th-century writer William Forster Lloyd's description of overgrazing on shared pastureland

explanations.text: The notes explicitly note the relevance to MAS and AI.
explanations.notes_quote: Tragedy-of-the-commons situations appear whenever many learning or planning agents share a limited resource and optimise only their own reward

explanations.text: The notes explicitly discuss its relevance to MAS and AI (shared bandwidth, autonomous vehicles, open multiagent platforms).
The notes list these as examples: overfishing, overgrazing, air pollution, deforestation.
explanations.notes_quote: Overfishing in international waters... Overgrazing on shared pastureland... Air pollution and greenhouse gas emissions

questions.text:
In the Coordination Game:

|  | L | R |
|---|---|---|
| **U** | $1, 1$ | $0, 0$ |
| **D** | $0, 0$ | $1, 1$ |

Which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The game has two pure strategy Nash equilibria: (U, L) and (D, R)
Both PSNE are also strong equilibria
Player 1 has a dominant strategy to play U
Neither player has a dominant or dominated strategy
This game has the same structure as the Prisoner's Dilemma

explanations.text: Both (U,L) and (D,R) are NE — neither player wants to deviate from either profile.
explanations.notes_quote: Two pure strategy Nash equilibria: (U, L) and (D, R)

explanations.text: Both PSNE are strong equilibria — no coalition has an improving deviation.
explanations.notes_quote: Both are also strong equilibria

explanations.text: Player 1 has no dominant strategy — playing U is only best when Player 2 plays L; D is best when Player 2 plays R.
explanations.notes_quote: No player has a dominant or dominated strategy

explanations.text: The notes explicitly state this.
explanations.notes_quote: No player has a dominant or dominated strategy

explanations.text: The Coordination Game has two NE and no dominant strategies — the PD has one NE with dominant strategies. They have different structures.

questions.text:
In the Battle of the Sexes:

|  | Theater | Football |
|---|---|---|
| **Theater** | $(2, 1)$ | $(0, 0)$ |
| **Football** | $(0, 0)$ | $(1, 2)$ |

Which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The game has two pure strategy Nash equilibria: (Theater, Theater) and (Football, Football)
BoS is a generalisation of a coordination game where players prefer coordinating but disagree on which outcome
The game has a unique Nash equilibrium (the MSNE only)
(Theater, Theater) gives payoffs $(2, 1)$, where the row player prefers Theater
In BoS, neither player benefits from coordinating on the same action

explanations.text: Both (Theater,Theater) and (Football,Football) are PSNE.
explanations.notes_quote: Two pure strategy Nash equilibria: (Theater, Theater) and (Football, Football)

explanations.text: This is how the notes describe BoS.
explanations.notes_quote: A generalisation of a coordination game where both players want to choose the same action, but have different preferences over possible consistent outcomes

explanations.text: BoS has TWO PSNE plus one MSNE — three Nash equilibria in total, not one.
From the payoff matrix, (Theater,Theater) gives $(2,1)$ — the row player gets 2, the column player gets 1.
Both players DO benefit from coordination — each prefers a coordinated outcome over miscoordination (payoff 0).

questions.text:
In the Matching Pennies game:

|  | H | T |
|---|---|---|
| **H** | $1, -1$ | $-1, 1$ |
| **T** | $-1, 1$ | $1, -1$ |

Which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Matching Pennies has no pure strategy Nash equilibrium
Matching Pennies is a zero-sum game
Player 1 wins (payoff $+1$) when both players choose the same side (H,H or T,T)
There exists a unique mixed strategy Nash equilibrium where each player randomizes 50-50
Player 2 wins when both players show Heads

explanations.text: There is a cycle of profitable deviations — no pure profile is stable.
explanations.notes_quote: No pure strategy Nash equilibrium exists (cycle of profitable deviations)

explanations.text: Payoffs always sum to 0: $1+(-1)=0$ and $(-1)+1=0$.
explanations.notes_quote: This is a zero-sum game

explanations.text: Player 1 is the 'matcher': they win when both show H or both show T (payoff +1).
The MSNE of Matching Pennies is each player playing H with probability 1/2 (shown in Section 6.6).
explanations.notes_quote: MSNE: $(\frac{1}{2}H + \frac{1}{2}T, \frac{1}{2}H + \frac{1}{2}T)$ with expected utility 0 for each player

explanations.text: Player 1 wins when both show Heads (H,H gives Player 1 payoff $+1$ and Player 2 payoff $-1$). Player 2 wins when they MISMATCH.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the Beauty Contest game? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Each participant chooses an integer from $\{0, 1, \ldots, 100\}$
The winner is the player whose strategy is closest to $\frac{2}{3}$ of the average of all strategies
Each player minimises $\left| s_i - \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{\sum_{j \in \mathbf{N}} s_j}{N} \right|$
The winning strategy is to always choose the maximum value of 100, since it anchors the average upwards
The Beauty Contest has no Nash equilibrium because the strategy set $\{0,\ldots,100\}$ is finite

explanations.text: The strategy set is the integers 0 through 100.
explanations.notes_quote: each participant $i$ chooses an integer from $S_i = \{0, 1, \ldots, 100\}$

explanations.text: The winning condition is to be closest to 2/3 of the average.
explanations.notes_quote: Player $i$ whose strategy $s_i \in S_i$ is closest to 2/3 of the average of all strategies wins the contest

explanations.text: This is the objective function each player minimises.
explanations.notes_quote: Each player's aim is to minimise: $\left| s_i - \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{\sum_{j \in \mathbf{N}} s_j}{N} \right|$

explanations.text: Choosing 100 is not a dominant winning strategy — a player's best choice depends on what others choose. Iterated reasoning drives choices toward 0.
By Nash's Theorem, every finite game (finite players, finite strategy sets) has at least one NE in mixed strategies.

questions.text:
In the Stag Hunt:

|  | Stag | Hare |
|---|---|---|
| **Stag** | $4, 4$ | $1, 3$ |
| **Hare** | $3, 1$ | $3, 3$ |

Which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: (Stag, Stag) and (Hare, Hare) are both pure strategy Nash equilibria
(Stag, Stag) with payoffs $(4, 4)$ is the payoff-dominant equilibrium
(Hare, Hare) with payoffs $(3, 3)$ is the risk-dominant equilibrium
Hunting Hare is a dominant strategy in the Stag Hunt
Hunting Stag is risky: if the other player hunts Hare, you only get 1, whereas Hare guarantees at least 3

explanations.text: Both profiles are Nash equilibria — no player wants to deviate at either.
explanations.notes_quote: Two PSNE: (Stag, Stag) and (Hare, Hare)

explanations.text: (Stag, Stag) gives the higher payoff $(4,4)$ and is payoff-dominant.
explanations.notes_quote: (Stag, Stag) is payoff-dominant but risky

explanations.text: (Hare, Hare) is the safer, risk-dominant equilibrium.
explanations.notes_quote: (Hare, Hare) is risk-dominant

explanations.text: Hare is NOT a dominant strategy. Stag is better when the opponent hunts Stag (4 vs 3). There is no dominant strategy in the Stag Hunt.
Stag gives 4 if the opponent hunts Stag but only 1 if they hunt Hare. Hare always gives 3 regardless — hence the risk.
explanations.notes_quote: (Stag, Stag) is payoff-dominant but risky

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe strict dominance? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Strategy $s$ strictly dominates $t$ for player $i$ if $U_i(s, s_{-i}) > U_i(t, s_{-i})$ for ALL $s_{-i} \in S_{-i}$
A player with a strictly dominant strategy should play it regardless of opponents' actions
Strict dominance requires that $s$ beats $t$ for at least one opponent strategy profile (not necessarily all)
In the Prisoner's Dilemma, Defect strictly dominates Cooperate
Strict dominance implies weak dominance

explanations.text: Strict dominance requires $s$ to be strictly better than $t$ for EVERY possible opponent strategy.
explanations.notes_quote: Strategy $s$ strictly dominates strategy $t$ if for every possible combination of strategies for other players $s_{-i} \in S_{-i}$: $U_i(s, s_{-i}) > U_i(t, s_{-i})$

explanations.text: This is the key strength of dominant strategies — no assumptions about opponents are needed.
explanations.notes_quote: A dominant strategy is a very strong solution concept — no assumptions needed about other players

explanations.text: Strict dominance requires $s$ to beat $t$ for ALL opponent strategies, not just some. That weaker condition is part of weak dominance.
In the PD, D gives $0 > -1$ (vs C) and $-5 > -10$ (vs D) — strictly better in both cases.
explanations.notes_quote: Dominant strategies do not often exist (they do in Prisoner's Dilemma)

explanations.text: If $>$ holds for all $s_{-i}$, then $\geq$ also holds for all $s_{-i}$ (and strictly for some), satisfying weak dominance.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe weak dominance? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Strategy $s$ weakly dominates $t$ if $U_i(s, s_{-i}) \geq U_i(t, s_{-i})$ for all $s_{-i}$ AND $U_i(s, s'_{-i}) > U_i(t, s'_{-i})$ for at least one $s'_{-i}$
Weak dominance requires strict inequality for EVERY opponent strategy profile
Every strictly dominated strategy is also weakly dominated
A weakly dominated strategy can appear in a Nash equilibrium
Weak dominance implies strict dominance

explanations.text: This is the exact definition from the notes: $\geq$ for all, and $>$ for some.
explanations.notes_quote: For all $s_{-i} \in S_{-i}$: $U_i(s, s_{-i}) \geq U_i(t, s_{-i})$; There exists some $s'_{-i}$: $U_i(s, s'_{-i}) > U_i(t, s'_{-i})$

explanations.text: Strict inequality is required only for AT LEAST ONE opponent strategy profile. Everywhere strict is the definition of STRICT dominance.
If $s$ strictly dominates $t$ ($>$ everywhere), then trivially $\geq$ everywhere and $>$ somewhere — so weak dominance holds.
Exercise 9.3b gives an example: A weakly dominates B, but (B, D) is still a Nash equilibrium.
explanations.section_ref: Section 9.3: Exercise 3: Weak Dominance and IEDS Order
explanations.notes_quote: A weakly dominates B, but (B, D) satisfies NE conditions (no player can profitably deviate)

explanations.text: It is the OPPOSITE: strict dominance implies weak dominance, not the other way around.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly distinguish dominant from dominated strategies? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A strategy is dominant for player $i$ if it dominates ALL other strategies of player $i$
A strategy is dominated if there EXISTS some other strategy that dominates it
Dominant strategies exist in most strategic games
A rational player will never choose a strictly dominated strategy
If player $i$ has a dominant strategy, they must know opponents' strategies to use it effectively

explanations.text: A dominant strategy must beat ALL other strategies of the same player.
explanations.notes_quote: Strategy $s$ is dominant for player $i$ if it dominates all other strategies $t$

explanations.text: A dominated strategy only needs to be beaten by at least one other strategy.
explanations.notes_quote: Strategy $t$ is dominated if there exists some strategy $s$ that dominates it

explanations.text: Dominant strategies do NOT often exist — the PD is a notable exception.
explanations.notes_quote: Dominant strategies do not often exist (they do in Prisoner's Dilemma)

explanations.text: Rational players never play strictly dominated strategies since there always exists a better option.
explanations.notes_quote: Rational players will never choose strictly dominated strategies

explanations.text: A dominant strategy is best REGARDLESS of what others do — knowing opponents' strategies is unnecessary.
explanations.notes_quote: A dominant strategy is a very strong solution concept — no assumptions needed about other players

questions.source_ref: Section 4.4: Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies (IEDS)
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe IEDS? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: For strict dominance, IEDS is path-independent: the order of elimination does not affect the outcome
For weak dominance, the outcome of IEDS may depend on the order of elimination
IEDS always produces a unique strategy per player, fully solving every game
A strategy that survives IEDS must be the dominant strategy for that player
Some games have no dominated strategies at all, and IEDS makes no progress

explanations.text: Path-independence is a key property of IEDS with strict dominance.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.4: Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies (IEDS)
explanations.notes_quote: For strict dominance: the outcome is path-independent (order of elimination doesn't matter)

explanations.text: Weak IEDS can yield different surviving strategy sets depending on elimination order (see Exercise 9.3).
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.4: Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies (IEDS)
explanations.notes_quote: For weak dominance: the outcome may depend on the order of elimination

explanations.text: IEDS may leave more than one strategy per player, or make no progress at all.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.4: Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies (IEDS)
explanations.notes_quote: We may end up with more than one strategy per player (game not fully solved)

explanations.text: The surviving strategy need not be dominant — it is simply not dominated by any remaining strategy.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.4: Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies (IEDS)
explanations.notes_quote: The surviving strategy need not be dominant

explanations.text: Some games have no dominated strategies and IEDS makes no eliminations.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.4: Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies (IEDS)
explanations.notes_quote: Some games have no dominated strategies at all

questions.text:
In the IEDS worked example (Exercise 1 from LGT):

|  | L | C | R |
|---|---|---|---|
| **T** | $73, 25$ | $57, 42$ | $66, 32$ |
| **M** | $80, 26$ | $35, 12$ | $32, 54$ |
| **B** | $28, 27$ | $63, 31$ | $54, 29$ |

Which statements about the IEDS process are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In Step 1, Player 2's strategy R strictly dominates L (since $32>25$, $54>26$, $29>27$), so L is eliminated
After eliminating L, both T and B dominate M for Player 1, so M is eliminated next
The IEDS solution is (T, C) with payoffs $(57, 42)$
After reaching the $2 \times 2$ game $\{T,B\} \times \{C,R\}$, Player 2's strategy C strictly dominates R
The final IEDS solution (B, C) is a Nash equilibrium

explanations.text: R gives Player 2 payoffs 32, 54, 29 vs L's 25, 26, 27 — strictly better in every row.
explanations.notes_quote: For Player 2, R dominates L (compare column by column: 32>25, 54>26, 29>27). Eliminate L.

explanations.text: In the $3 \times 2$ subgame, T gives Player 1 (57,66) vs M's (35,32) and B gives (63,54) vs M's (35,32) — both T and B beat M.
explanations.notes_quote: For Player 1, T and B both dominate M. Eliminate M.

explanations.text: The IEDS solution is (B, C) with payoffs $(63, 31)$, not (T, C). B dominates T in the final $2 \times 1$ column.
explanations.notes_quote: Solution: (B, C) with payoffs $(63, 31)$

explanations.text: In the $2 \times 2$ subgame, C gives Player 2 payoffs 42 and 31 vs R's 32 and 29 — strictly better in both rows.
explanations.notes_quote: For Player 2, C dominates R (42>32 and 31>29). Eliminate R.

explanations.text: The IEDS solution (B, C) is also a Nash equilibrium — each player is playing their best response.
explanations.notes_quote: (B, C) is a Nash equilibrium: each player is playing their best response to the other player's strategy

questions.text: Which of the following statements about the IEDS result (B, C) from the worked example are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: At the solution (B, C), Player 1 earns $63$ and Player 2 earns $31$
Because strict IEDS is path-independent, eliminating strategies in a different valid order would yield the same result (B, C)
Player 2's strategy R is eliminated in Step 3 because C strictly dominates R in the reduced game
B is a dominant strategy for Player 1 in the original $3 \times 3$ game
IEDS with strict dominance guarantees a path-independent result

explanations.text: From the payoff matrix, (B, C) gives payoffs $(63, 31)$.
explanations.notes_quote: Solution: (B, C) with payoffs $(63, 31)$

explanations.text: Path-independence of strict IEDS guarantees the same result regardless of elimination order.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.4: Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies (IEDS)
explanations.notes_quote: For strict dominance: the outcome is path-independent (order of elimination doesn't matter)

explanations.text: After eliminating L and M, Step 3 compares C and R for Player 2: C gives 42>32 and 31>29.
explanations.notes_quote: For Player 2, C dominates R (42>32 and 31>29). Eliminate R.

explanations.text: B is NOT dominant in the original $3 \times 3$ game — against L, T gives 73 > B's 28. B only dominates T in the reduced game after M and L are removed.
Path-independence is a key property of strict IEDS.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.4: Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies (IEDS)
explanations.notes_quote: For strict dominance: the outcome is path-independent (order of elimination doesn't matter)

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe unilateral improving deviations? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Player $i$ has a unilateral improving deviation from profile $s$ if $\exists s'_i \in S_i$ with $U_i(s'_i, s_{-i}) > U_i(s_i, s_{-i})$
A unilateral deviation involves multiple players changing strategies simultaneously
A profile with no unilateral improving deviations for any player is a Nash equilibrium
Unilateral improving deviations always exist in finite strategic games
Unilateral improving deviations capture the core idea behind Nash equilibrium stability

explanations.text: This is the formal definition: player $i$ can do strictly better by switching to $s'_i$ while others stay fixed.
explanations.notes_quote: player $i$ has a unilateral improving move (deviation) if there exists some $s'_i \in S_i$ such that $U_i(s'_i, s_{-i}) > U_i(s_i, s_{-i})$

explanations.text: A UNILATERAL deviation involves ONLY ONE player changing strategy — all other players' strategies remain fixed.
This is the definition of Nash equilibrium — no player has an incentive to deviate unilaterally.
explanations.notes_quote: A pure strategy Nash equilibrium is a strategy profile $s^*$ where no player has a unilateral improving move

explanations.text: Nash equilibrium profiles have NO unilateral improving deviations by definition — they are stable precisely because no player can improve.
NE is defined as the absence of unilateral improving deviations — no player benefits from deviating alone.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Pure Strategy Nash Equilibrium (PSNE)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A PSNE $s^*$ satisfies $U_i(s^*_i, s^*_{-i}) \geq U_i(s'_i, s^*_{-i})$ for all $s'_i \in S_i$ and all $i \in \mathbf{N}$
In a PSNE, every player plays a best response to the strategies of all other players
A dominant strategy equilibrium is NOT a Nash equilibrium
A PSNE requires each player to have a uniquely optimal strategy — ties are not allowed
A PSNE is a profile from which no player has a unilateral improving move

explanations.text: This is the formal NE condition: each player's strategy achieves the maximum payoff given others' strategies.
explanations.notes_quote: $U_i(s^*_i, s^*_{-i}) \geq U_i(s'_i, s^*_{-i}) \quad \forall s'_i \in S_i, \forall i \in \mathbf{N}$

explanations.text: NE means each player is best-responding to the others.
explanations.notes_quote: every player plays their best response to the strategies of other players

explanations.text: Every dominant strategy equilibrium IS a Nash equilibrium — dominant strategies are a special case of best responses.
explanations.notes_quote: Every dominant strategy equilibrium is a Nash equilibrium

explanations.text: The NE condition uses $\geq$, not $>$. Ties are allowed — a player can be indifferent between strategies and still be in equilibrium.
This is an equivalent definition of NE.
explanations.notes_quote: A pure strategy Nash equilibrium is a strategy profile $s^*$ where no player has a unilateral improving move

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe properties of PSNE? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A PSNE is not necessarily unique — games can have multiple pure strategy Nash equilibria
Every finite game has at least one PSNE
Every dominant strategy equilibrium is a Nash equilibrium
Nash equilibrium is a WEAKER solution concept than surviving IEDS
Some games have no PSNE at all (e.g., Matching Pennies)

explanations.text: For example, the Coordination Game and Professor's Dilemma each have two PSNE.
explanations.notes_quote: Not necessarily unique: Coordination game has two PSNE

explanations.text: Matching Pennies has NO pure strategy Nash equilibrium — finite games are only guaranteed a MIXED strategy NE (by Nash's Theorem).
explanations.notes_quote: May not exist: Matching Pennies has no PSNE

explanations.text: Dominant strategies are best responses to ALL opponent strategies, so certainly best responses to any specific opponent strategy.
explanations.notes_quote: Every dominant strategy equilibrium is a Nash equilibrium

explanations.text: Nash equilibrium is a STRONGER requirement than surviving IEDS — every NE survives IEDS but not every IEDS survivor is a NE.
explanations.notes_quote: Nash equilibrium is a stronger requirement than surviving IEDS

explanations.text: Matching Pennies has a cycle of profitable deviations and no stable pure profile.
explanations.notes_quote: May not exist: Matching Pennies has no PSNE

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe mixed strategies? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A mixed strategy is a probability distribution over the set of pure strategies
A pure strategy is a special case of a mixed strategy (probability 1 on one action, 0 on all others)
With $n$ pure strategies, there are exactly $n$ possible mixed strategies
$\Delta_i$ denotes the set of all probability distributions (mixed strategies) for player $i$
Mixed strategies are only relevant in games where no pure strategy Nash equilibrium exists

explanations.text: This is the definition of a mixed strategy.
explanations.notes_quote: A mixed strategy is a probability distribution over the set of pure strategies

explanations.text: A pure strategy assigns probability 1 to one action — it is a degenerate mixed strategy.
explanations.notes_quote: A pure strategy is a special case of a mixed strategy (one action played with probability 1)

explanations.text: With $n$ pure strategies, there are INFINITELY MANY mixed strategies (any probability distribution over $n$ outcomes).
explanations.notes_quote: Even with finite pure strategy sets, there are infinitely many mixed strategies

explanations.text: $\Delta_i$ is the notation for the set of mixed strategies for player $i$.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $\Delta_i$ denote the set of probability distributions over $S_i$ (the set of mixed strategies for player $i$)

explanations.text: Mixed strategies are always defined, even in games with PSNE. PSNE are special cases of MSNE.

questions.source_ref: Section 6.2: Expected Utility under Mixed Strategies
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe expected utility under a mixed strategy profile $\tilde{s}$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $U_i(\tilde{s}) = \sum_{s \in S} \left(\prod_{j \in \mathbf{N}} \tilde{s}_j(s_j)\right) U_i(s)$
Players' mixed strategies are correlated when computing expected utility under a mixed profile
The expected utility sums over ALL pure strategy profiles $s \in S$
$\tilde{s}_j(s_j)$ is the probability that player $j$ plays pure strategy $s_j$ under their mixed strategy
Each pure strategy profile's contribution is weighted by the probability that all players simultaneously choose those strategies

explanations.text: This is the exact formula for expected utility under a mixed strategy profile.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.2: Expected Utility under Mixed Strategies
explanations.notes_quote: $U_i(\tilde{s}) = \sum_{s \in S} \left(\prod_{j \in \mathbf{N}} \tilde{s}_j(s_j)\right) U_i(s)$

explanations.text: Players' strategies are INDEPENDENT — the formula uses the product $\prod$ of individual probabilities, not a correlated distribution.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.2: Expected Utility under Mixed Strategies

explanations.text: The outer sum is over the full space $S$ of all pure strategy profiles.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.2: Expected Utility under Mixed Strategies

explanations.text: $\tilde{s}_j(s_j)$ is the probability player $j$ assigns to pure strategy $s_j$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.2: Expected Utility under Mixed Strategies

explanations.text: The product $\prod_j \tilde{s}_j(s_j)$ is the probability of profile $s$ occurring when all players mix independently.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.2: Expected Utility under Mixed Strategies

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium (MSNE)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: An MSNE is a mixed strategy profile where each player's strategy is a best response to the others
An MSNE requires every player to randomize uniformly over all their pure strategies
Every PSNE is also an MSNE (as a degenerate mixed strategy)
The MSNE condition is $U_i(\tilde{s}^*_i, \tilde{s}^*_{-i}) \geq U_i(\tilde{s}'_i, \tilde{s}^*_{-i})$ for all $\tilde{s}'_i \in \Delta_i$
An MSNE can only exist when no PSNE is present in the game

explanations.text: MSNE generalises the NE concept to mixed strategies — each player's mixed strategy is a best response.
explanations.notes_quote: a mixed strategy Nash equilibrium is a mixed strategy profile $\tilde{s}^*$ where each player's mixed strategy is a best response to the others

explanations.text: An MSNE only requires that each player's strategy be a best response — not that randomization be uniform. Probabilities are determined by the indifference principle.
A pure strategy is a degenerate mixed strategy, so every PSNE satisfies the MSNE conditions.
This is the formal definition of MSNE.
explanations.notes_quote: $U_i(\tilde{s}^*_i, \tilde{s}^*_{-i}) \geq U_i(\tilde{s}'_i, \tilde{s}^*_{-i}) \quad \forall \tilde{s}'_i \in \Delta_i, \forall i \in \mathbf{N}$

explanations.text: MSNE exist in all finite games (Nash's Theorem). Games WITH PSNE also have MSNE representations.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Nash's Theorem? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Nash's Theorem guarantees that every finite game has at least one Nash equilibrium in mixed strategies
Nash's Theorem was proved by John Nash in 1950
Nash's Theorem applies only to two-player zero-sum games
Nash's Theorem guarantees the uniqueness of Nash equilibria in finite games
Nash's Theorem requires both the number of players and the action sets to be finite

explanations.text: Nash's Theorem guarantees existence (not uniqueness) of at least one NE in mixed strategies.
explanations.notes_quote: Every game with a finite set of players and finite sets of actions has at least one Nash equilibrium in mixed strategies

explanations.text: The theorem was proved by John Nash in his 1950 paper.
explanations.notes_quote: Theorem (Nash, 1950)

explanations.text: Nash's Theorem applies to ANY finite game with any number of players — not just two-player zero-sum games.
Nash's Theorem only guarantees EXISTENCE of at least one NE, not uniqueness. Games can have multiple NE.
The theorem requires 'a finite set of players and finite sets of actions'.
explanations.notes_quote: Every game with a finite set of players and finite sets of actions has at least one Nash equilibrium in mixed strategies

questions.source_ref: Section 6.5: Computing Mixed Nash Equilibria: The Indifference Principle
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the Indifference Principle for computing MSNE? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A player randomizes between strategies if and only if they are indifferent between those strategies
Player $i$'s mixing probabilities are chosen to make the OTHER player(s) indifferent
If a player is indifferent between two strategies, they must mix over them with equal probability $\frac{1}{2}$ each
In an MSNE, every pure strategy in a player's support yields the same expected utility
The indifference principle states that a player randomizes to maximise their OWN expected payoff

explanations.text: A player only randomizes when they are indifferent — otherwise they would play the better strategy with probability 1.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.5: Computing Mixed Nash Equilibria: The Indifference Principle
explanations.notes_quote: A player will randomize between strategies if and only if they are indifferent between those strategies

explanations.text: Player $i$'s mixing probabilities are set to make the OTHER player(s) indifferent — this is the key insight.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.5: Computing Mixed Nash Equilibria: The Indifference Principle
explanations.notes_quote: Player $i$'s mixing probabilities are determined by making the other player indifferent

explanations.text: Indifference does not force equal probabilities. The specific mixing probabilities are determined by making the OTHER player(s) indifferent, not by equal-weighting.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.5: Computing Mixed Nash Equilibria: The Indifference Principle

explanations.text: All strategies in a player's support must yield equal expected utility — otherwise the player would shift probability to the better strategy.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.5: Computing Mixed Nash Equilibria: The Indifference Principle

explanations.text: A player randomizes to make the OTHER player indifferent (and thus willing to mix), not to directly maximise their own payoff.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.5: Computing Mixed Nash Equilibria: The Indifference Principle

questions.text: In the MSNE computation for Matching Pennies (Player 1 plays H with prob $p$, Player 2 plays H with prob $q$), which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The MSNE has each player playing H with probability $\frac{1}{2}$
To find $p$, we set Player 2's expected utility from H equal to their expected utility from T
Setting Player 2 indifferent: $-p + (1-p) = p - (1-p)$ gives $p = \frac{1}{2}$
The MSNE gives each player an expected payoff of $0$
Player 1 determines their mixing probability $p$ by solving for what makes THEMSELVES indifferent

explanations.text: By symmetry, both players mix 50-50 in the MSNE.
explanations.notes_quote: MSNE: $(\frac{1}{2}H + \frac{1}{2}T, \frac{1}{2}H + \frac{1}{2}T)$ with expected utility 0 for each player

explanations.text: Player 1's probability $p$ makes Player 2 indifferent — we set $U_2(H) = U_2(T)$ to find $p$.
explanations.notes_quote: For Player 2 to be indifferent between H and T: $U_2(H) = U_2(T)$

explanations.text: This algebra is shown step by step in the notes and gives $p = 1/2$.
At the 50-50 MSNE, both players' expected payoffs are 0.
explanations.notes_quote: expected utility 0 for each player

explanations.text: Player 1's probability $p$ is determined by making PLAYER 2 indifferent, not Player 1. Player 2's probability $q$ makes Player 1 indifferent.

questions.text: In the Stag Hunt MSNE computation (Player 1 plays Stag with prob $p$, Player 2 with prob $q$), which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The MSNE has each player playing Stag with probability $\frac{2}{3}$
Setting Player 2 indifferent: $4p + (1-p) = 3p + 3(1-p)$ gives $p = \frac{2}{3}$
If Player 2's payoff from (Stag, Hare) changes from $1$ to $2$, it is Player 2's mixing probability $q$ that changes
The Stag Hunt has exactly three Nash equilibria: (Stag, Stag), (Hare, Hare), and the MSNE
Player 1's mixing probability $p$ makes Player 1 indifferent between Stag and Hare

explanations.notes_quote: Each player plays Stag with probability $2/3$ and Hare with probability $1/3$

explanations.text: This is the algebra for making Player 2 indifferent between Stag and Hare.

The notes explicitly state it affects PLAYER 1's mixing probability: changing Player 2's payoff at (Stag, Hare) changes what makes Player 2 indifferent, which changes $p$ (Player 1's probability), not $q$.
explanations.notes_quote: If Player 2's payoff from (Stag, Hare) changes from 1 to 2, it affects Player 1's mixing probability (changes to $p = 1/2$), not Player 2's

explanations.text: The two PSNE plus one MSNE give three total Nash equilibria.
Player 1's probability $p$ makes PLAYER 2 indifferent. Player 2's probability $q$ makes Player 1 indifferent — this is the key insight of the indifference principle.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.5: Computing Mixed Nash Equilibria: The Indifference Principle
explanations.notes_quote: Player $i$'s mixing probabilities are determined by making the other player indifferent

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the maximin (safety level) strategy? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The maximin strategy maximises player $i$'s worst-case payoff
The maximin formula is $\max_{s_i \in S_i} \min_{s_{-i} \in S_{-i}} U_i(s_i, s_{-i})$
The maximin strategy assumes all other players will try to minimise player $i$'s payoff
The maximin strategy always coincides with the dominant strategy
The safety level value is the guaranteed minimum payoff from playing the maximin strategy

explanations.text: Maximin maximises over own strategies the minimum payoff achievable against the worst-case opponent.
explanations.notes_quote: The maximin (or safety level) strategy for player $i$ maximises the worst-case payoff

explanations.text: This is the formal maximin formula.
explanations.notes_quote: $\max_{s_i \in S_i} \min_{s_{-i} \in S_{-i}} U_i(s_i, s_{-i})$

explanations.text: The maximin models a pessimistic adversarial scenario — opponents act to minimise your payoff.
explanations.notes_quote: assuming all other players play to cause the greatest harm to player $i$

explanations.text: The maximin strategy is a safety strategy, not necessarily a dominant one. They coincide in the PD but not in general.
The safety level value is exactly the payoff guaranteed by playing the maximin strategy.
explanations.notes_quote: The maximin value (safety level value) is the guaranteed minimum payoff from playing this strategy

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the minimax strategy? (Select ALL that apply.)

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the Support Lemma? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $x_1 \in B_1(x_2^*)$ if and only if every pure strategy in the support of $x_1$ is itself a best response to $x_2^*$
If some $s$ in the support of $x_1$ has $u_1(s, x_2^*) < \alpha$ (the maximum achievable utility), then $x_1$ cannot be a best response
The Support Lemma implies mixed strategies are never best responses when a pure best response exists
If every pure strategy in the support yields the same maximum utility $\alpha$, then any mixture over them also achieves $\alpha$
The Support Lemma is irrelevant to the computation of mixed strategy Nash equilibria

explanations.text: This is the statement of the Support Lemma.
explanations.notes_quote: $x_1 \in B_1(x_2^*)$ if and only if every pure strategy $s \in S$ in the support of $x_1$ is itself in $B_1(x_2^*)$

explanations.text: If any support strategy yields less than $\alpha$, the mixture's expected utility is less than $\alpha$ — contradiction.
explanations.notes_quote: suppose for contradiction that some $s$ in the support has $u_1(s, x_2^*) < \alpha$. Then $u_1(x_1, x_2^*) < \alpha$ — Contradiction

explanations.text: The Support Lemma actually ALLOWS mixed best responses — any mixture over strategies all achieving $\alpha$ is also a best response.
If all support strategies yield $\alpha$, any convex combination also yields $\alpha$.
explanations.notes_quote: If every pure strategy in the support yields $\alpha$: $u_1(x_1, x_2^*) = \sum_{s} x_1(s) \alpha = \alpha$. So $x_1 \in B_1(x_2^*)$

explanations.text: The Support Lemma IS the theoretical foundation for the Indifference Principle, which is used to compute MSNE.

explanations.notes_quote: Rational players will never choose strictly dominated strategies

questions.text: Which of the following correctly identify limitations of Nash equilibrium as a solution concept? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Nash equilibria are not necessarily unique — some games have multiple NE (e.g., the Coordination Game)
Pure strategy Nash equilibria may not exist in some games (e.g., Matching Pennies has no PSNE)
Nash equilibrium outcomes may be Pareto-inefficient — the PD NE is Pareto-dominated
Every finite game has a unique Nash equilibrium in mixed strategies
A unique Nash equilibrium is always Pareto-optimal

explanations.text: Multiplicity of NE is a key limitation — games often have many equilibria.
explanations.notes_quote: Not necessarily unique: Coordination game has two PSNE

explanations.text: Non-existence of PSNE is another limitation — only MSNE existence is guaranteed for finite games.
explanations.notes_quote: May not exist: Matching Pennies has no PSNE

explanations.text: The PD NE (D,D) is Pareto-dominated by (C,C) — NE does not guarantee efficiency.
explanations.notes_quote: The equilibrium (D,D) with payoffs $(P,P)$ is Pareto-dominated by (C,C) with payoffs $(R,R)$

explanations.text: Nash's Theorem guarantees EXISTENCE of at least one NE, but NOT uniqueness. Battle of the Sexes has three NE.
The Prisoner's Dilemma has a UNIQUE NE (D,D) that is Pareto-suboptimal — uniqueness does not imply efficiency.
explanations.notes_quote: Individual rationality leads to a collectively suboptimal outcome
